note: Original sample questions written for this open-source demo, in the style of the production question bank. The production deployment points this server at the private question-bank store (2,500+ taxonomically classified problems); real past-paper content is licensed and is not redistributed here.
hint_policy: Three-tier pedagogy mirroring the production RAG hint system: hint_1 nudges, hint_2 gives the approach, solution shows full working.

questions.subject: maths
questions.question: What is the smallest positive integer that is divisible by both 6 and 8?
questions.hint_1: Is it really 6 × 8 = 48? Can you find something smaller that both divide into?
questions.hint_2: List the multiples of 8 (8, 16, 24, ...) and stop at the first one that is also a multiple of 6 — this is the lowest common multiple.
questions.solution: The LCM of 6 and 8: 6 = 2 × 3 and 8 = 2³, so LCM = 2³ × 3 = 24.

questions.subject: maths
questions.question: How many two-digit numbers have digits that add to 9?
questions.hint_1: Write a few down: 18, 27... what pattern do the first digits follow?
questions.hint_2: The tens digit can run from 1 to 9; the units digit is forced each time. Check the edge case ending in 0.
questions.solution: Tens digit t runs 1-9 with units digit 9−t: 18, 27, 36, 45, 54, 63, 72, 81, 90 — nine numbers.

questions.subject: maths
questions.question: What is the last digit of 7^2026?
questions.hint_1: Work out the last digit of 7¹, 7², 7³, 7⁴ — what do you notice?
questions.hint_2: The last digits cycle 7, 9, 3, 1 with period 4, so reduce the exponent modulo 4.
questions.solution: Last digits of powers of 7 cycle (7, 9, 3, 1). 2026 mod 4 = 2, so the last digit matches 7², which is 9.

questions.subject: maths
questions.question: A square has the same area as a 16 cm by 4 cm rectangle. What is the perimeter of the square?
questions.hint_1: Start from what the two shapes share — the area.
questions.hint_2: Area = 64 cm², so find the side length of the square first, then its perimeter.
questions.solution: Rectangle area = 16 × 4 = 64 cm². Square side = √64 = 8 cm, so perimeter = 4 × 8 = 32 cm.

questions.subject: maths
questions.question: If x + y = 10 and xy = 21, what is x² + y²?
questions.hint_1: You don't need to find x and y individually.
questions.hint_2: Expand (x + y)² and rearrange for x² + y².

questions.subject: maths
questions.question: In how many ways can the letters of MATHS be arranged so that the arrangement starts with a vowel?
questions.hint_1: How many vowels does MATHS have? What does fixing the first letter leave you with?
questions.hint_2: Fix A in the first position, then count arrangements of the remaining four distinct letters.
questions.solution: The only vowel is A. Fix A first; the remaining 4 distinct letters arrange in 4! = 24 ways.

questions.subject: maths
questions.question: How many positive integers less than 100 have exactly three positive divisors?
questions.hint_1: Try small numbers: which ones have exactly three divisors? What kind of numbers are they?
questions.hint_2: A number has exactly three divisors precisely when it is the square of a prime (divisors: 1, p, p²).
questions.solution: Squares of primes under 100: 4, 9, 25, 49 (11² = 121 is too big) — four numbers.

questions.subject: maths
questions.question: A circle is inscribed in a square of side 10. What is the area inside the square but outside the circle? Give your answer in terms of π.
questions.hint_1: What is the radius of a circle inscribed in a square of side 10?
questions.hint_2: Subtract the circle's area from the square's area; the circle's diameter equals the square's side.
questions.solution: Radius = 5, so circle area = 25π. Square area = 100. Region = 100 − 25π.

questions.subject: maths
questions.question: In how many ways can 3 identical apples be shared among 4 children, if a child may receive none?
questions.hint_1: The apples are identical — only how many each child gets matters.
questions.hint_2: Count solutions of a + b + c + d = 3 in non-negative integers (stars and bars).

questions.subject: maths
questions.question: One tap fills a tank in 6 hours and a second tap fills it in 3 hours. Running together, how many minutes do they take to fill the tank?
questions.hint_2: Add the two rates (1/6 + 1/3 of a tank per hour), then invert.
questions.solution: Combined rate = 1/6 + 1/3 = 1/2 tank per hour, so the tank fills in 2 hours = 120 minutes.
